title: Twillingate
content:
We all end up badly and
        it's not the season nor the salt
        rather, I suspect but type of gherkin used.

        We all end, badly, at least
        the more modest of us do.
        the old salts they dine on
        limericks anyways.

        We all end up, sadly, the distances
        and the wiles only last up,
        sideways, and barely with
        the edge-ways of a smile.

        Some of us, sadly,
        limit our losses
        call off the posse
        quit deals, the
        quicksilver steals.

        Some of us, gladly,
        surrender or catch
        a slow boat to Twillingate,
        if not willingly,
        at least painstakingly.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Paul Cameron Brown, titled "Twillingate", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
